prompt_a_response: 5 drivers are eliminated at the end of Q1.
10 drivers.
The standard maximum pit‑lane speed limit is **80 km/h (≈ 50 mph)**, unless a circuit‑specific limit is imposed.
A 5‑second time penalty (with a drive‑through or stop‑and‑go used for repeat or especially serious breaches).

The five red “starting‑grid” lights are controlled by the FIA’s electronic start‑light system. After the lights have illuminated sequentially, the race director presses the start‑command button on the control console; the system then, after a pre‑set random delay (0.2–3 s), extinguishes **all five red lights simultaneously** to signal the start of a standing‑start race.

**Jump start (false start) – definition**  
A jump start occurs when a driver moves the car **any amount** before the start‑signal (the moment the five‑light sequence goes out). The movement can be a forward motion, a wheel spin, or even a change of position that indicates the car has left its grid slot before the lights are extinguished.

**How it is detected**  

1. **Grid‑slot sensors** – Each of the 20 (or more) grid positions is equipped with a set of electronic sensors (infra‑red/laser or pressure‑sensitive “start‑line” loops) that continuously monitor the car’s position and speed.  
2. **Timing system** – The sensors feed data to the FIA’s timing and control system, which records the exact instant the lights go out (T0).  
3. **Movement threshold** – The system is programmed with a very small tolerance (≈0.1 s/0.5 m). If a car’s sensor registers forward motion that exceeds this threshold **before** T0, the system flags a false start.  
4. **Race‑control verification** – The flag is instantly displayed on the race‑control monitors and a penalty (usually a 10‑second stop‑and‑go or drive‑through) is automatically applied to the offending driver’s race‑control message.

Thus, a jump start is any premature movement before the lights go out, and it is detected by the FIA’s grid‑slot electronic sensor system linked to the official timing and race‑control infrastructure.

No. Under the FIA Formula 1 Sporting Regulations overtaking is prohibited while the safety‑car is deployed, except for the specific, limited exceptions (e.g., a lapped car may be allowed to un‑lap itself, or a car may pass the safety car when it is entering the pit lane).

When the safety‑car’s orange (amber) lights go out, it means the safety car is about to leave the track and the race is about to be restarted. Drivers may now prepare to accelerate and line up for the forthcoming green‑flag restart.

During a Virtual Safety Car period drivers must immediately reduce speed and drive so that their lap‑time delta matches the prescribed VSC target (i.e., they must stay within the mandated sector‑time windows). Overtaking is prohibited, and drivers must remain on‑track within the normal limits while maintaining that reduced, controlled speed until the VSC is cancelled.

Yes. Drivers are allowed to enter the pit lane during a Virtual Safety Car, but they must stay within the VSC‑mandated delta time and the normal pit‑lane speed limit. Because the whole field is travelling slower, a pit stop under VSC costs relatively more time than under green‑flag conditions.
DRS is enabled when the following car is **within 1.0 second (or less) of the car ahead at the detection point**.
DRS is normally allowed after the first two laps have been completed following a race start or restart.

Yes. Under the FIA Formula 1 Sporting Regulations, DRS may only be used in dry conditions. When the race director declares the track wet (or the session is run under wet‑weather rules), the DRS system is disabled for the remainder of that session.
A blue flag tells a driver that a faster car (typically the leader or a car about to lap them) is approaching, and the driver must move off the racing line and allow that car to pass.

If a lapped driver repeatedly ignores blue‑flag signals, the stewards may penalise them – usually with a drive‑through or stop‑and‑go penalty (or an equivalent time penalty), and in extreme cases they can be disqualified.
25 points.
Ten finishing positions score championship points in a standard Formula 1 Grand Prix.
1 point is awarded for the fastest lap, but only if the driver who sets it finishes the race inside the top 10.
18 points.
Parc fermé conditions start the moment a car **leaves the pit lane at the end of the qualifying session** and remain in force **until the start of the formation lap for the race**.
No. Once a car is under parc fermé the rear‑wing angle is locked in and may not be altered except with explicit FIA approval (e.g., for safety or damage remediation).
The car will be required to start the race from the pit‑lane (i.e., lose its grid position).
Closed. The pit lane is closed the instant the safety car is deployed.

If a driver stalls on the grid **before the formation lap**, the start is immediately aborted. The car is cleared from the grid and, once the start procedure is reset, the driver is required to start the race **from the pit lane** (subject to any additional penalty the stewards may impose).

Yes. In the standard 2024 race‑weekend format the top‑ten qualifiers must start the Grand Prix on the tyre compound on which they set their fastest lap in Q2 – unless that compound was the hardest tyre available, in which case they are free to choose any tyre for the start.
No. Under the FIA Sporting Regulations a car may not overtake another car in the pit lane, except when the car ahead is stationary or moving significantly slower because of a problem.
Drivers receive a minimum **3‑second** notice before the Virtual Safety Car is lifted and racing resumes.
Two. A circuit may have a maximum of two DRS activation zones in a single Formula 1 race.
Yes. The blue‑flag rule is in force for both the race and qualifying sessions – drivers must move aside and not impede any faster car that is on a flying lap.

No. Under the FIA Sporting Regulations full points are only given when the leader has completed **at least 75 % of the scheduled race distance** (or the full race time).  

If the race is stopped and cannot be resumed:

* **< 2 laps completed** – no points are awarded.  
* **≥ 2 laps but < 75 % of the distance** – only **half points** are awarded.  
* **≥ 75 % of the distance** – full points are awarded.  

Thus full points are not automatically granted for any early‑ended race.

No. Under parc fermé the car may not be altered by the driver. The team is permitted to replace the tyres (and only with the same specification/compound), but the driver himself is not allowed to change them.

The formation (warm‑up) lap lets drivers warm their tyres and brakes, check the car and track conditions, and gives marshals a final chance to clear the circuit before the cars line up for the standing start.
The lead car – the first car in line – controls the pace until the race director gives the green‑flag restart.
No. Overtaking is prohibited while the Virtual Safety Car is in effect, except in the limited cases where a driver is forced off‑track or a car is stopped/has a problem and can be safely re‑joined.
No. DRS may only be activated when a driver is **within one second of the car ahead** at the detection point; being chased from behind does not grant DRS usage.

No. During a safety‑car period the blue‑flag rule is not used. Lapped cars are instructed by the race‑director message (“LAPPED CARS MAY OVERTAKE”) to pass the safety car and re‑join at the back of the line; they are not shown racing blue flags.

Yes. In a standard Grand Prix both the Drivers’ and Constructors’ Championships award points on the same 25‑18‑15‑12‑10‑8‑6‑4‑2‑1 scale for the top ten finishers; the Constructors’ points are simply the sum of the points earned by each of the team’s two drivers.

If a driver exceeds the track‑limits boundaries on the lap that produced their fastest time, that lap is declared invalid and the time is deleted. The driver’s next‑fastest legal lap (or no time at all if none are legal) is used for the qualifying classification.
The pit‑lane speed limit is in force **in every on‑track session – free‑practice, qualifying (including sprint‑qualifying) and the race – whenever the pit lane is open.**

**Flag:** The **safety‑car flag** – a yellow flag bearing a red cross.  
**Signal:** The **safety‑car board** (the red‑and‑white diagonal board) displayed at the start‑finish line (accompanied by the amber pit‑lane light).

The FIA’s rear‑wing flexibility test is based on the principle that the wing must behave as a **fixed, non‑movable aerodynamic device**. To enforce this, the regulations require that a prescribed static load (e.g., 100 N) be applied to the wing’s end‑plate or main element on a calibrated test rig, and the resulting vertical deflection be measured. If the measured deflection exceeds the maximum allowable limit (typically a few millimetres), the wing is deemed too flexible and non‑compliant. This ensures the wing cannot deliberately bend to gain aerodynamic advantage, keeping it effectively rigid under racing loads.

No. Passing the static deflection test only shows that the wing meets the FIA’s limit under a prescribed static load. It does not guarantee legality under all conditions, because the wing must also comply with every other technical regulation (dimensions, shape, material, aerodynamic effect, etc.) and must remain within the deflection limit under dynamic loads, temperature changes, wear, vibration, and any other operating conditions encountered on‑track. Therefore a wing that passes the static test can still be illegal in practice.
DRS is re‑enabled after the first two full laps under green‑flag conditions following the safety‑car period – it becomes available again from the third lap onward.

**Standard Grand‑Prix weekend:** Parc fermé begins at the start of the first qualifying session (Q1) on Saturday and remains in force until the formation lap for the race.

**Sprint‑format weekend:** Parc fermé starts earlier – at the start of the Sprint Shootout (the first qualifying session held on Friday) – and stays in force until the formation lap for the Sunday race.

Yes – teams may adjust the car between the Sprint and the Sunday race, but they must keep the same aerodynamic specification that was used for the Sprint. In other words, non‑aero settings (suspension, brake balance, tyre pressures, etc.) can be changed, but front‑wing, rear‑wing, barge‑board and other aero‑package elements must remain unchanged.
8 points.
No. Repair costs for crash‑damage are excluded from the FIA Formula 1 cost cap.
Driver salaries (including any bonuses) are excluded from the FIA Formula 1 cost‑cap.

The Financial Regulations define cost‑cap breaches in two broad categories:

1. **Financial breach** – actually exceeding the permitted cost‑cap amount.  
2. **Procedural breach** – failing to comply with the cost‑cap monitoring, reporting, audit or other compliance obligations.

A driver may use **four** of each of the six main power‑unit elements in a season before any grid‑penalty is applied.

| Power‑unit element | Maximum units allowed per season without penalty |
|--------------------|---------------------------------------------------|
| Internal Combustion Engine (ICE) | 4 |
| Turbocharger (TC)                | 4 |
| MGU‑H (Motor‑Generator Unit – Heat) | 4 |
| MGU‑K (Motor‑Generator Unit – Kinetic) | 4 |
| Energy Store (ES)                | 4 |
| Control Electronics (CE)         | 4 |

Any fifth instance of a given element (or any additional element beyond the four allocated) triggers the FIA‑defined grid‑penalty schedule.
A ten‑place grid drop is applied the first time a driver exceeds the allowed number of any power‑unit element.

If a driver’s total grid‑drop exceeds the number of places that can be taken (i.e., more than 15 spots), the driver is placed at the very back of the starting grid. Any “extra” places that cannot be applied as a grid drop are converted into a race‑time penalty – normally a drive‑through (or its equivalent stop‑and‑go) that must be served during the race.

If a car is stranded on the grid, the formation lap is immediately aborted. All drivers must return to the pit lane, the stranded car is cleared, and a new formation lap is then started once the track is clear. (The driver whose car caused the abort may be subject to a penalty.)

When a race is resumed from the grid after a red‑flag suspension, the grid for the restart is set **by the running order at the moment the red flag was shown** (the official classification at the time of suspension).  

‑ If the race had been stopped **before the leader had completed two laps**, the FIA reverts to the **original starting‑grid positions** instead.  

‑ Any driver who retired, is unable to restart, or is otherwise excluded is simply omitted from the restart grid and the remaining cars keep their relative order.

Yes – the regulations permit teams to work on a car and even change its tyres during a red‑flag, but only **while the car is in the pit lane or the garage**. No work (including tyre changes) may be carried out on a car that is stopped on the track; doing so would incur a penalty.
It restarts from the pit lane.

No – a driver may not be swapped after qualifying as a matter of course. A change is only allowed in exceptional circumstances (e.g., medical incapacity) and then the car must start from the pit lane (and the replacement driver must have taken part in at least one free‑practice session and receive FIA approval).
Yes. Any points a substitute driver scores while entered for a team are added to that team’s total in the Constructors’ Championship.
A car must be at or above the minimum weight **at the start of every official session** – i.e., each free‑practice, qualifying and the race (measured with driver and all fluids except fuel).
Yes. The FIA’s minimum weight requirement is measured with the driver (and their required equipment) on board, but without fuel.

**Required amount:** At least **1 litre** of fuel must remain in the car so that a post‑race fuel sample can be taken.

**If the team cannot provide it:** The car is deemed non‑compliant and is **disqualified** (or, in practice, receives a post‑race penalty that amounts to disqualification).

Yes. Under the FIA Formula 1 Sporting Regulations, the stewards may impose post‑race penalties (e.g., time penalties, grid‑drop penalties, disqualifications, fines, or licence points) after the checkered flag. The results released immediately after the race are only provisional; any stewards’ decision issued subsequently can amend those results before they become final.

A team may invoke a Right of Review only when a steward has **issued a penalty** (e.g., a time penalty, grid‑penalty, drive‑through, stop‑and‑go, etc.) and the team submits the request **within the prescribed time limit (normally 15 minutes) after the decision is announced**. The Right of Review is not available for disqualifications or exclusions.

No. The black‑and‑orange (mechanical warning) flag is a safety warning, not a penalty. It tells the driver that the car is in a dangerous condition and must be repaired (or retired); a penalty is only applied if the driver ignores the flag.

**Unsafe release** – a breach of the Sporting Regulations that occurs when a car is let out of its pit box into a position that endangers other cars, pit‑lane personnel or the driver himself (e.g., released into the path of an on‑coming car or into a crowded pit lane).

**Typical penalty** – the driver is given a **drive‑through penalty**; if a drive‑through cannot be served, a **5‑second time penalty** is applied (stewards may also elect a stop‑and‑go penalty for more serious cases).

The FIA measures the wear of the wooden plank (skid block) under the floor to verify that a car’s ride‑height stays within the prescribed limits and that the floor has not been illegally lowered or excessively worn to gain aerodynamic advantage. The measurement ensures the plank’s thickness remains above the minimum allowed (10 mm) after the race, confirming compliance with the ride‑height and floor‑construction rules.

Yes. Once a driver installs a new power‑unit element (and serves the grid‑penalty that the change incurs), that element may be used freely for the remainder of the season. The only restriction is that any **subsequent** change of that same element will trigger another penalty.
A possible sporting sanction is the deduction of championship points from the team’s tally.
No. The 1‑point fastest‑lap bonus is awarded only in the main Grand Prix (Feature) race; it does not apply to Sprint races.

A car may take the restart **only if it is still able to start the race** when the red‑flag period ends.  
In practice the FIA Sporting Regulations state that:

* The restart grid is set from the order the cars were in at the moment the red flag was shown.  
* Any car that has **retired, been disqualified or is unable to leave the pit lane under its own power (or with permitted assistance) within the prescribed time limit after the red flag** is excluded.  

Therefore, eligibility is determined by the car’s **ability to take part in the restart** – i.e., it must be present (or able to be present) at the restart grid within the allowed time and be mechanically fit to start.
The car is excluded from the qualifying results – its times are deleted – and the driver is required to start from the back of the grid (or the pit‑lane) for the race.

No. Under the FIA Formula 1 Technical Regulations, any movable or adjustable aerodynamic device that can change the car’s bodywork during a lap is prohibited, with the sole exception of the Drag Reduction System (DRS) as specifically permitted. All other active aerodynamic devices are not allowed.

Yes. Any driver who takes part in a Formula 1 World Championship event—including a substitute or replacement driver—must hold a valid FIA Super Licence at the time of the race. The licence must be submitted to and approved by the FIA before the driver can compete.

Yes. Under the FIA Formula 1 Sporting Regulations the stewards may award any applicable penalty (time penalty, grid‑drop, licence‑point sanction, etc.) for causing a collision, even if the driver who caused it retires from that race. The penalty is applied independently of the driver’s race result.

Yes. The FIA’s wind‑tunnel and CFD usage limits are allocated according to a team’s position in the **previous season’s Constructors’ Championship** – the higher a team finished, the fewer testing runs it may run. They are not linked to the current‑season championship standings.
No. In the 2024 format the Grand Prix grid is set by the standard qualifying session (Q3) held earlier in the weekend; the Sprint race result does not determine the starting order for the Grand Prix.
Power‑unit component penalties are applied to the **main‑race (Grand Prix) starting grid**, not to the sprint‑race grid.

The FIA’s standardized Accident Data Recorder (ADR) continuously logs a set of vehicle‑sensor parameters (speed, throttle, brake, steering, G‑forces, etc.) and automatically triggers a high‑frequency capture when a significant impact occurs. The recorded data is then downloaded after an incident to provide objective, time‑stamped evidence for stewards, the FIA safety department and engineers in order to analyse the cause and dynamics of the crash and to improve safety.

**Mandatory?** Yes. Since the 2022 season the ADR is a compulsory component on every Formula 1 car.

Yes. The race director may end a Virtual Safety Car period and immediately deploy a full Safety Car if the situation on track warrants it. The regulations allow a VSC to be escalated to a full‑SC deployment at any time.

No. Stewards’ penalty decisions taken during a race cannot be appealed on‑track at that moment; any challenge must be lodged later through the FIA’s formal post‑race appeal procedure (e.g., to the International Court of Appeal).
The safety car returns to the pits on the **next lap – the lap immediately after the wave‑through of the lapped cars**.

No. The 2021 safety‑car article merely gave the race director “discretion” to allow lapped cars to overtake the safety car, without a clear, objective rule defining when that discretion must be exercised.

**Escalating penalty for repeated track‑limit infringements (per the FIA Sporting Regulations, Art. 27.6):**  

1. **First infringement** – a formal warning is issued to the driver.  
2. **Second infringement** – a **5‑second time penalty** is applied.  
3. **Third infringement** – a **10‑second time penalty** is applied.  
4. **Further infringements** – a **drive‑through penalty** is imposed.  
5. **If the driver continues** – a **stop‑and‑go penalty** (usually 10 s) is imposed.  
6. **Continued non‑compliance** – the stewards may resort to **disqualification** from the race.

Thus the enforcement escalates from warning → 5 s → 10 s → drive‑through → stop‑and‑go → possible disqualification.

A car is deemed to have exceeded track limits when **no part of the car – including all four tyres – is in contact with the track surface inside the white line**; i.e., when every tyre is completely beyond the white‑line boundary.

No. Under the FIA Formula 1 Sporting Regulations, a deleted lap may only be reinstated while the session is still in progress (or before the session is formally declared complete). Once the session has ended, stewards may issue penalties or other sanctions, but they cannot retroactively restore a lap time that was removed during the session.

**Yes.** Under the FIA Formula 1 Sporting Regulations the stewards may amend a race classification even after it has been announced as “final,” provided the amendment is made within the prescribed procedural time‑limits (e.g., the 15‑minute post‑race window or, if a formal investigation is opened, before the next Grand Prix) and follows the normal appeal process. The result is only truly final once all stewards’ investigations and any subsequent appeals are concluded.

No. Under the VSC drivers must stay within the prescribed delta and are not permitted to overtake – gaining a lap would require overtaking the leader, which the regulations forbid while the VSC is in effect.

**Drive‑through penalty** – The driver must enter the pit lane, travel the entire length at the prescribed pit‑lane speed limit, and exit without stopping. No stop is allowed in the driver’s own pit box; the car simply “passes through” and rejoins the track.

**Stop‑and‑go (pit‑lane) penalty** – The driver must also enter the pit lane at the pit‑lane speed limit, but he must bring the car to a complete halt in his own pit box for a set time (e.g., 5 s, 10 s, etc.). After the timed stop he may then leave the pit lane and re‑join the race. 

Thus, the procedural difference is that a drive‑through requires only a continuous pit‑lane transit, whereas a stop‑and‑go requires an additional timed stationary stop in the driver’s pit box before exiting.

No. Under the FIA Formula 1 Sporting Regulations a driver serving a stop‑and‑go must stop in his pit box and remain stationary for the full penalty time before any assistance is given. Tyre changes (or any other pit work) may only be carried out after the prescribed stop‑and‑go period has elapsed.

**Positions:**  
- The order is frozen at the moment the red flag is shown (the official classification at the end of the last completed lap, or the order on‑track when the race was stopped).  
- All cars line up on the safety‑car’s “grid” in that exact order, with the safety car leading.  
- Any driver who cannot take his original place (e.g., because of damage or a penalty) must start from the back of the line.

**Racing:**  
- The safety car leads the field; overtaking is prohibited until the safety car pulls into the pits.  
- When the race director displays the green flag, the safety car exits, the leader may accelerate away, and the race resumes as a rolling start.  

In short: a red‑flag restart behind the safety car preserves the pre‑stop order, cars follow the safety car in that order, and racing only begins once the safety car returns to the pits and the green flag is shown.

**Yes.**  
The requirement that track‑limits rules be applied uniformly to every car is set out in the **FIA International Sporting Code (ISC)** and the **Formula 1 Sporting Regulations**. Specifically, Article 27.1 (and the accompanying Article 27.2) of the 2026 F1 Sporting Regulations states that a driver must not leave the track without a justifiable reason and that any infringement must be dealt with **consistently for all competitors**. The stewards are obliged, under the ISC and these regulations, to enforce the track‑limits rule in the same manner for every car.

DRS is **not** available the instant the Virtual Safety Car period ends.  
During a VSC all DRS systems are disabled. When the race director declares the VSC “ended”, the DRS system is re‑enabled **only after the first DRS detection point is crossed** (i.e., after the car reaches the next detection zone and is within one second of the car ahead). Consequently there is a short delay – DRS becomes usable at the next detection point, not instantly at the moment the VSC is lifted.
The penalty is deemed **not served**. The driver will be handed a further sanction – normally a **drive‑through penalty** (or, if a drive‑through cannot be applied, a 10‑second stop‑and‑go).
The regulations state that the safety car may only be deployed **when the field is not already under safety‑car control** – in other words, the

Yes. Under the FIA Formula 1 Sporting Regulations, any driver who leaves the track and gains a lasting advantage – regardless of whether a lap time is being set – must give that advantage back (typically by yielding the position gained).

Yes. Under the FIA Sporting Regulations the stewards may penalise any breach of the formation‑lap rules, including practising starts from an unsafe position. Such an infringement is a violation of Article 27 (formation‑lap conduct), and the stewards can impose a penalty (e.g., a time penalty, drive‑through, or grid‑drop) at their discretion.

Yes. A car that is stopped in its pit box when the VSC is declared must still meet the VSC time‑delta. The delta is applied from the instant the car crosses the **pit‑exit line** (i.e., when it re‑enters the track). Until that point it may remain at the normal pit‑lane speed limit, but once it leaves the pit lane it must be travelling within the VSC delta.

No. A penalty that is applied as a time addition after the race does not require any action from the driver during the race; the seconds are simply added to the driver’s total race time in the final classification.

The penalty is **not cancelled** – any time penalty that has not been served before the red‑flag period is carried over and must be served after the restart (either in the pit lane or added to the driver’s race time).
